key_facts: Largest land carnivore of its time
Bite force of 12,800 newtons
Fossils found in Hell Creek Formation (Montana, Wyoming)
Walked on two powerful hind legs with small arms
Had excellent vision and could run up to 25 mph
Had distinctive plates along its back for display or thermoregulation
Four spikes on tail used for defense
One of the most recognizable dinosaurs
Brain was approximately the size of a walnut
Likely used plates to attract mates during breeding season
Three horns and a large bony frill on its head
Frill was used for species recognition and defense
One of the last dinosaurs to exist before extinction
Could run up to 20 mph despite its size
Horns were up to 1 meter long
One of the largest land animals ever to exist
Front legs were longer than hind legs
Could reach heights up to 13 meters to feed on treetops
Heart estimated to weigh 400 kg
Weighed as much as 17 African elephants
Smaller than often depicted in movies
Had large curved claw on each foot for hunting
Likely hunted in packs
Was incredibly fast and agile
Had excellent binocular vision for a dinosaur
Covered in armor plating made of bone and horn
Had a club-like tail used for defense
Armor was effective against predator attacks
Very low center of gravity for stability
Could not be easily turned over by other dinosaurs
Had a distinctive curved crest on its head
Crest was hollow and used for vocalization
Could make low frequency sounds for communication
Lived in herds with other hadrosaurs
Crest resembled a question mark when viewed from the side
One of the first dinosaurs formally recognized and named
Could walk on two or four legs
Had a thumb spike possibly used in defense
Teeth similar to modern iguanas
Was an important genus for understanding dinosaur evolution
Was semi-aquatic, spending time in water
Sailed-like structures on back were made of tall neural spines
Larger than T-Rex when including the sail
Hunted fish and large dinosaurs
First dinosaur known to hunt in water
Name means 'terrible claw'
Had exceptional hunting abilities and intelligence
Could run at speeds over 50 km/h
Hunted in packs to take down larger prey
Likely one of the smartest dinosaurs

Had feathers, wings, and clawed fingers
Could not fly effectively, likely glided
Teeth and bony tail similar to theropod dinosaurs
One of the most important fossils in paleontology
Formerly known as Brontosaurus
One of the largest land animals ever
Long whip-like tail used for balance and communication
Would have needed to eat constantly to fuel its body
Had a very small brain relative to body size
Apex predator of the Jurassic period
Sharp curved claws on hands and feet
Could likely reach speeds of 20-30 km/h
May have hunted cooperatively with others
Had better binocular vision than most dinosaurs
Resembled an ostrich more than a traditional dinosaur
Could run at speeds exceeding 50 km/h
Used its tail as a rudder while running
Had a toothless beak for eating varied foods
Likely lived in herds for protection
Had two large horns above its eyes
Bull-like appearance gave it its name
Could move quickly despite its size
Horns likely used in intraspecific combat

Had a horn on its nose similar to a modern rhinoceros
Smaller than T-Rex but very powerful predator

Strong forelimbs with large claws

Relatively small compared to later carnivores

Was about the size of a modern chicken
Extremely fast and agile for hunting small prey
Had sharp teeth and claws for catching lizards and insects
Some of the finest fossils ever found
Small herbivorous dinosaur
Had opposable big toes for climbing
Could run at speeds up to 30 km/h
Lived in forested environments
Teeth adapted for chewing tough plant material
One of the earliest dinosaurs ever discovered
Had both carnivorous and herbivorous teeth
Small, bipedal predator-scavenger
Lived during the early days of dinosaur evolution

Spikes along shoulders for defense

Lived in herds for group protection
Synapsid, not a true dinosaur but often confused
Lived before dinosaurs evolved
Had thick skull adapted for head-butting
Ancestor to mammals
Robust build with powerful hind legs

Lived in large herds
Cared for young in nests
Had complex social structures
Larger than T-Rex
Fast and powerful predator
Possibly hunted in groups
Had sharp teeth and claws

Fed on soft ferns, conifers, and mosses
Could elevate its neck to about 45 degrees
Lived in massive herds for protection against allosaurs
Had a crocodile-like snout and specialized cone teeth for catching fish

Related to the giant Spinosaurus
Famous for its thick, domed skull bone up to 25 cm thick
Dome was surrounded by small bony spikes and bumps
Likely engaged in head-butting or flank-butting combat
Had tiny, ridged teeth suited for soft leaves and fruits
Coexisted with Triceratops and Tyrannosaurus rex
Had the longest claws of any animal in history, up to 1 meter long
Claws were used for defense and pulling down high branches
A giant herbivore belonging to the theropod (typically meat-eating) group
Possessed a small head, long neck, and a wide, bulky body
Name means 'scythe lizard' due to its giant claws
Sheep-sized early ceratopsian dinosaur without horns
Had a prominent bony frill on the back of its skull
A famous fossil preserves a Protoceratops locked in combat with a Velociraptor
Possessed a strong beak for chewing tough desert vegetation
Lived in large herds and nested in sandy desert hollows
The official state dinosaur of New Jersey
First dinosaur skeleton to be mounted for public display
A duck-billed dinosaur with hundreds of self-sharpening teeth

Helped establish that dinosaurs were active land animals

Crest was hollow and connected to nasal passages for vocal calls
Crest may have also been brightly colored for mating display
Duck-billed dinosaur with flat beak and chewing battery
Many well-preserved mummified skin impressions have been found
One of the earliest and most successful small dinosaurs
Lightweight, hollow bones made it extremely fast and agile
Hundreds of skeletons found together at Ghost Ranch, New Mexico
Had large eyes, blade-like teeth, and three-clawed hands
Likely hunted small mammals, reptiles, and insects
Sauropod dinosaur with two parallel rows of long spines on its neck
Spines were extensions of the vertebrae, up to 60 cm tall
Spines may have supported a sail or been used for display and defense
Relatively short neck and small size for a sauropod
Fossils discovered in Neuquén Province, Argentina
Widely considered the largest and heaviest land animal ever to live
A single vertebra measured over 1.5 meters tall
Traveled in slow-moving herds to search for vast quantities of foliage
Young hatched from eggs the size of coconuts
Only incomplete skeletons have been found, leaving exact size disputed
Name means 'egg thief' due to first fossil found on top of dinosaur eggs
Later discoveries proved it was actually brooding its own nest
Possessed a short, toothless beak and a small head crest
Had feathered body, long legs, and curved hand claws
Likely ate seeds, nuts, insects, and small lizards
A tiny dromaeosaur with feathers on both its arms and legs
Functioned as a four-winged glider, steering between forest trees
Fossil analysis shows iridescent black feathers, like a modern crow
Preyed on small birds, mammals, fish, and lizards
Key evolutionary link showing dinosaur-to-bird transition
Named 'fearing nothing' due to its colossal, invulnerable adult size
One of the most complete titanosaure skeletons ever discovered
Had a massive 9-meter neck and a muscular 9-meter tail
Spent all day grazing to maintain its huge body mass
Fossils recovered from Patagonia, Argentina
Pterosaur, not a dinosaur, but the largest flying animal of all time
Had a wingspan of 10 to 11 meters, equivalent to a small airplane
Stood as tall as a modern giraffe when walking on the ground
Used a long, toothless beak to hunt small land animals and fish
Walked on all fours using its folded wing joints
Marine reptile, not a dinosaur, with a long snake-like neck
Used four large, paddle-like flippers to fly through marine waters
Had needle-sharp teeth adapted for trapping slippery fish and squid
Famously served as the design inspiration for the Loch Ness Monster myth
Gave birth to live young rather than laying eggs on land
Apex marine reptile predator, not a dinosaur, of the late oceans
Had a heavy skull, double-hinged jaw, and rows of conical crushing teeth
Propelled through water using a powerful, shark-like tail fin
Preyed on ammonites, fish, turtles, and even smaller mosasaurs
First specimen discovered in a limestone quarry in Maastricht, Netherlands
Giant pliosaur marine reptile with a massive 2.5-meter long skull
Had teeth as large as bananas, exceeding the size of T-Rex teeth
Used short neck and powerful flippers for ambush underwater attacks
Named after the Greek Titan Cronus who ate his own children
Top ocean predator of the Early Cretaceous seas
A synapsid reptile, more closely related to mammals than dinosaurs
Lived millions of years before the first dinosaur evolved
Famous for the massive neural spine sail on its back
Sail was likely used for thermoregulation and mating display
Had two distinct types of teeth for shearing flesh and bone
Pterosaur with a large bony head crest extending backwards
Had a wingspan of up to 6 meters, but very lightweight skeleton
Toothless beak used to scoop up fish from ocean surface
Crest may have served as a rudder or visual sexual display
Fossils frequently found in marine deposits far from coastlines
One of the largest duck-billed dinosaurs, competing in length with T-Rex
Had a soft, fleshy comb on its head, similar to a rooster
Possessed a massive dental battery containing over 1,000 grinding teeth
Could move on two legs or crawl slowly on four
Coexisted with Triceratops and was a primary prey of T-Rex
The Asian cousin of Tyrannosaurus rex, slightly smaller but equally deadly
Had the smallest arms of any large tyrannosaurid dinosaur
Equipped with a rigid locking jaw mechanism to secure large prey
Apex predator of the Nemegt Basin in Mongolia
Lacked the binocular vision of T-Rex, relying more on smell and hearing
The largest raptor (dromaeosaurid) to ever walk the earth
Equipped with massive, curved 22 cm sickle claws on its second toes
Weighed as much as a grizzly bear, built far more robustly than Velociraptor
Preyed on large iguanodonts and sauropods
Walked on two toes, keeping the giant sickle claw raised off the ground
Small, fast, bipedal dinosaur built for speed
Had large eyes, indicating excellent vision for spotting predators
Had a horny beak and cheek pouches to store food during chewing
Lacked armor, relying entirely on running speed for defense
Fossils found in the Morrison Formation
Spiked relative of Stegosaurus discovered in Tendaguru, Tanzania
Had small plates on neck, shifting to long spines along back and tail
Possessed two extremely long spikes projecting outwards from its shoulders
Low-slung body adapted for grazing on ground ferns and low foliage
Could swing its spike-armored tail in a wide arc for self-defense
Name means 'parrot lizard' due to its short, curved beak skull shape
An early ancestor of the horned ceratopsian dinosaurs
Some fossils preserve bristles resembling stiff quill hairs on its tail
Gastroliths (gizzard stones) have been found in rib cages, used to grind seeds
One of the most species-rich dinosaur genera, with over 10 named species
The largest known dinosaur with direct fossil evidence of feathers
Name translates to 'beautiful feathered tyrant'
Possessed long, shaggy proto-feathers used for insulation in cool climates
An early relative of Tyrannosaurus rex
Hunted in the colder forested regions of Liaoning, China
